title: Body trust scan
subtitle: A short evening meditation for noticing the body's automatic competence and handing sleep back to it.
excerpt: A ten-minute meditation from Day 17 of the SLEEP program. The practice moves attention through the body's automatic processes — breathing, heartbeat, digestion — and uses them as evidence that sleep can be trusted to happen the same way.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Body scan method draws on widely established mindfulness practice; sleep-specific framing adapted from Day 17 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use as part of the evening wind-down, particularly when the urge to manage or monitor sleep is strong.

content.text: A ten-minute evening meditation from Day 17 of the SLEEP program. The premise: your body is already running many complex processes without your direction. Sleep is one of them. This practice uses the others as evidence.
Lie down or sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Let your body settle for a moment before you begin.

Bring attention to your breath. Notice that you don't have to make it happen. It was happening before you noticed it. It will keep happening when your attention moves elsewhere. You can observe it, but it doesn't need you to manage it.

Now bring attention to your heartbeat. Find it if you can — in your chest, your wrists, your throat. It has been beating since before you were born. It speeds up and slows down based on signals you never consciously send. It does this reliably, all night, while you sleep. You are not in charge of it. It works without you.
Turn attention to your belly. Your digestion is working right now. Sorting, moving, processing. You gave it no instructions. It knows what to do.

Stay with that knowledge for a moment. Your body is running breathing, circulation, and digestion simultaneously, without your involvement, right now. These are not simple tasks. It manages them without effort from you.

Sleep is the same kind of process. Your body knows the mechanics. It knows when to shift from light sleep to deep, how to move through cycles, when to surface. It has done this for your whole life. The thing that interrupts it is not a lack of knowledge in your body — it is the attempt to direct something that works better when left alone.

Let your attention rest in the body without directing anything. Breath. Heartbeat. Warmth. Weight. When you notice the urge to check or manage something, return to the breath and let the body carry on.
Stay here for several minutes. Then let yourself drift, or open your eyes.

Original to homemade.education. Body scan method draws on widely established mindfulness practice; sleep-specific framing adapted from Day 17 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
